name: Circus Maximus
description: The Circus Maximus, Rome's largest chariot racing stadium, held immense significance in Roman life, far beyond mere entertainment. Originally constructed in the valley between the Aventine and Palatine Hills, it evolved over centuries from a simple track to a monumental structure capable of holding hundreds of thousands of spectators. Chariot races were not only thrilling spectacles but also deeply intertwined with Roman religion and politics. Victories were seen as signs of divine favor, and the races themselves were often dedicated to various gods. The Circus Maximus was also used for other public events like gladiatorial combats (before the Colosseum's construction), animal hunts, and public executions. The space became a focal point for collective identity and social interaction, a place where Romans from all social classes could come together to celebrate their shared culture. Its association with the founding myths of Rome, particularly the abduction of the Sabine women during a festival held at the Circus, cemented its place in the Roman psyche. The Circus Maximus remained a vital part of Roman life for centuries, its echoes resonating even today as a symbol of Roman power and entertainment. It was a stage for grand spectacles, political maneuvering, and the display of Roman values, all under the watchful eyes of the gods.
subtitle: Rome's grand chariot racing stadium

keyMyths.title: The Abduction of the Sabine Women
keyMyths.description: Romulus, needing wives for his new city, invited the neighboring Sabines to a festival at the Circus Maximus. During the games, the Roman men abducted the Sabine women, leading to a war that ultimately ended with the two groups merging.

keyMyths.title: Games Dedicated to Consus
keyMyths.description: The Circus Maximus was dedicated to the god Consus, protector of grain storage. Underground altars to Consus were discovered there, and the Consualia festival, involving chariot and mule races, was held to honor him and ensure a bountiful harvest.

sources.author: Livy
sources.description: A comprehensive history of Rome from its founding to the reign of Augustus.
sources.text: A comprehensive history of Rome from its founding to the reign of Augustus.
